topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like interrogation lamps, exposing every clumsy brushstroke, every hesitant line. I stood near the entrance, my hands clenched so tightly my knuckles were white islands in my skin. The canvases, vibrant explosions of color I had poured my soul into, hung there, untouched by my gaze. A sharp, unwelcome laugh echoed from a group of well-dressed patrons. They circled the pieces, their murmurs appreciative, distant. I watched them, a statue carved from anxiety, feeling the familiar, cold weight settle in my chest. Every glance felt like a judgment, every appreciative nod a confirmation that I was utterly insignificant. I wanted to melt into the polished floor, to become invisible, to erase the evidence of my existence.

[story 2]

"Oh, this piece is certainly… bold," a voice drifted over. I flinched, my shoulders hunching inward as if bracing for a physical blow. I kept my eyes fixed on the floor, tracing the pattern of the expensive rug with my worn-out shoes. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a humiliating flush that spread down my neck. They were admiring my work, yes, but it felt like they were admiring the scaffolding, not the structure. I shuffled toward the back wall, pretending to examine a smudge on the frame, desperate for a distraction, a way to disappear before the scrutiny became unbearable. The silence of my own inadequacy roared louder than any applause.

[story 3]

I watched from across the room as someone pointed, a gesture so casual, so dismissive, that it felt like a physical shove. My stomach twisted into a tight, nauseous knot. I forced a small, brittle smile, a mask that felt glued to my face. Inside, a torrent of self-reproach flooded me—all the nights spent staring at blank canvases, the crippling fear that nothing I created held any value. The air in the room suddenly felt too thin, suffocating. I took a slow, deliberate sip of water, the coolness doing nothing to soothe the internal tremor shaking my hands. I just wanted the entire exhibition to dissolve, to vanish, leaving no trace of the effort I had expended.
